sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a 72-year-old woman who presents for follow-up for hypertension.
She reports struggling with not drinking and has been drinking four to five times a week.
She has been taking half the dose of lisinopril due to side effects, but has not experienced any symptoms on the full dose of hydrochlorothiazide.
She denies chest tightness, shortness of breath, or any other symptoms.
She is taking her blood pressure medications as prescribed, but is taking them in the morning instead of the evening.
She has seen lower blood pressure readings in the morning compared to the evening.
She has been vaccinated against the coronavirus.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure was not measured during the visit, but she reports seeing readings of 130-145 over 95-100 in the morning and 150 over 100 in the evening.
She has a regular cardiac rhythm and no murmur or gallop.
Her skin is moist and good.
Her head is at 90 degrees with a flat JVP.
Test Results:
CMP and CBC were essentially unremarkable at her last visit.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has alcohol use disorder, which is contributing to her hypertension.
Problem list: Alcohol use disorder; Hypertension; Active; Active; Past: Mild hypercholesterolemia.
Plan:
I prescribed naltrexone as an injectable form to be administered once a month to help with alcohol cessation.
The potential benefits of alcohol cessation include improved blood pressure control.
I will see her back in the week of June 14th, which is likely around the time of her second naltrexone injection.
I recommend upgrading her influenza vaccination and Shingrix vaccination, which she has deferred.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient again in the week of June 14th to follow up on her progress with naltrexone and to check her blood pressure.
